title: Sri Lankan Egg Hoppers

ingredients.name: rice flour, fine
visual_checks: Use fine-ground white rice flour; do not substitute with coarse rice flour.
ingredients.notes: Use fine-ground white rice flour; do not substitute with coarse rice flour.

subs.name: Equal volume of fermented dosa or idli batter — omit baking soda and sugar, as fermentation provides natural lift and tang.

ingredients.name: coconut milk, full-fat
visual_checks: Divide: 250 ml thick for the batter, 150 ml to adjust consistency.
ingredients.notes: Divide: 250 ml thick for the batter, 150 ml to adjust consistency.

subs.name: 50 g coconut milk powder dissolved in 400 ml warm water; the hoppers will be slightly less rich but still lacy and crisp at the edges.

ingredients.name: baking soda
visual_checks: Provides the lift that fermentation would normally give; do not increase or the hoppers will taste soapy.
ingredients.notes: Provides the lift that fermentation would normally give; do not increase or the hoppers will taste soapy.

ingredients.name: sugar
visual_checks: A small amount activates the baking soda and adds a subtle sweetness to the crepe.
ingredients.notes: A small amount activates the baking soda and adds a subtle sweetness to the crepe.

ingredients.name: eggs (one per hopper)
visual_checks: Use at room temperature for even setting.
ingredients.notes: Use at room temperature for even setting.

subs.name: Skip entirely for plain hoppers (appa); the batter alone makes beautiful lacy crepes — serve with a generous spoonful of pol sambol.

ingredients.name: cooking oil or coconut oil, for greasing
visual_checks: Coconut oil gives the most authentic aroma.
ingredients.notes: Coconut oil gives the most authentic aroma.

steps.text: Combine rice flour, baking soda, sugar, and salt in a large bowl. Whisk in 250 ml thick coconut milk gradually, working out any lumps. The batter should be smooth and slightly thicker than dosa batter — add thin coconut milk (or water) a tablespoon at a time until it flows off a spoon in a slow ribbon.

Cover the batter and rest for 20 minutes at room temperature. After resting, the batter will loosen slightly. Stir and check the consistency again — it should coat the back of a spoon but still flow easily.

Heat a small, deep-sided kadai or curved frying pan (10–12 cm base diameter) over medium heat. Lightly oil the pan using a folded paper towel dipped in oil or a pastry brush. The pan is ready when a drop of batter sizzles immediately on contact.

Pour 4–5 tbsp of batter into the hot pan. Immediately grasp the pan by the handle and swirl in a swift circular motion so the batter coats the sides thinly all the way up, leaving a small pool at the base. Crack one egg directly into the centre pool, cover with a tight-fitting lid, and cook for 2–3 minutes until the egg white is completely set and the edges of the hopper are dry and slightly pulling away from the pan.

Run a thin spatula around the edges to loosen the hopper, then ease it out onto a plate — it should release cleanly, lacy and golden at the rim. Repeat for the remaining batter, re-oiling between each. Serve immediately with pol sambol, coconut dhal (parippu), and seeni sambol.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
critical_checks: Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: rice-flour → Equal volume of fermented dosa or idli batter — omit baking soda and sugar, as fermentation provides natural lift and tang.; coconut-milk → 50 g coconut milk powder dissolved in 400 ml warm water; the hoppers will be slightly less rich but still lacy and crisp at the edges.; egg → Skip entirely for plain hoppers (appa); the batter alone makes beautiful lacy crepes — serve with a generous spoonful of pol sambol.
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Use a small kuzhi karandi (the traditional South Indian appam pan) or a deep-sided tawa; any small wok with curved sides works. The batter is almost identical to Kerala-style appam batter — Kerala appam batter mix from Indian grocery stores is an excellent shortcut (just add egg to each). Fermented dosa batter thinned with coconut milk is a readily available substitute. Serve with coconut chutney if pol sambol ingredients are unavailable.
